title: Why Reuse a Trained Network?
concept: Transfer learning starts a new task from a model already trained on a large dataset, so you reuse useful learned features instead of forcing a network to rediscover them from scratch.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What kind of thing do deep networks learn inside their hidden layers when training goes well?

options.text: Only random numbers with no pattern
A fixed list of labels with no weights

exercises.explain: Exactly. Those learned features are what transfer learning hopes to reuse.

cards.title: Do not rebuild the whole toolbox
cards.body: If someone already spent months building a great toolbox, you would not melt the metal back down just to make one screwdriver. Transfer learning asks the same question about neural networks: if a model has already learned useful visual or language patterns, why start from zero?

cards.title: What transfer learning means
cards.body: Transfer learning means starting a new task from a pretrained model instead of random weights. The earlier training, often done on a much larger dataset, gives the network a head start because many learned features are still useful on the new task.

cards.title: Hiring a chef, not teaching knife skills first
cards.body: If a chef already knows chopping, seasoning, and timing, a new restaurant only needs to teach its menu. A pretrained network is similar: the model may already know broad patterns like edges, textures, shapes, or common word relationships, so the new task can focus on its specialty.

cards.title: From everyday photos to medical images
cards.body: Suppose you want to classify X-ray images but only have a modest labeled dataset. A vision model first trained on millions of general images may already know how to detect lines, blobs, and structures. Transfer learning lets you reuse that foundation instead of asking a small medical dataset to teach every low-level pattern from scratch.

cards.title: Reuse is not the same as memorizing the old task
cards.body: A common myth is that a pretrained model can only repeat its original labels. In reality, transfer learning reuses the features and weights the model learned, not just its final answers. Those features can still help on a different but related task.

cards.title: From scratch vs. pretrained start
cards.body: Both approaches can learn, but one begins with far more prior experience.

left.text:
Random starting weights
Must learn basic + task-specific patterns all at once
Usually needs more data and time

right.text:
Starts from pretrained weights
Reuses useful learned features
Usually learns faster on the new task

exercises.prompt: Why is transfer learning especially attractive when your new labeled dataset is small?

options.text: Because the pretrained model already brings useful features from earlier training
Because small datasets make random initialization more accurate by itself
Because transfer learning removes the need for any training labels
Because pretrained models never need an output layer

exercises.explain: Correct. Reusing learned features can reduce how much new data is needed to get started.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Transfer learning only works if the new task has exactly the same labels as the original task.
exercises.explain: False. The labels can change. What transfers is the learned representation, not just the old label names.

exercises.sentence: Starting from a ___ model often gives a new task a head start.
exercises.explain: Yes. A pretrained model already contains useful structure from earlier learning.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario is the clearest example of transfer learning?

options.text: Using a model first trained on millions of photos as the starting point for a five-flower classifier
Deleting all learned weights before every training run
Using a spreadsheet to sort files by date

exercises.explain: Right. The key clue is reusing a pretrained network as the starting point for a new task.

exercises.prompt: A wildlife group has only 800 labeled camera-trap images but wants a species classifier quickly. What is the strongest first strategy?

options.text: Start from a pretrained vision network and adapt it to the wildlife labels
Train a deep network from random weights because pretrained features would never help animals
Avoid neural networks because transfer learning only works for text
Keep the old model exactly unchanged and never connect it to the new labels

exercises.explain: Exactly. With limited data, borrowing a pretrained visual foundation is often the practical move.
